description: Rework the voice of the 4 Guide docs pages (plugin-development, error-handling, streaming-progress, logging-debugging) to match the personality and genuine warmth of the README. Design decisions should feel like personal stories, not documentation. Tradeoffs should be honest. The author's personality should be present in every section intro.
qualityChecks: cd docs && npm run build && npm run type-check && npm run lint && npm run knip && npm run format:check

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/guides/plugin-development.mdx voice
userStories.description: The plugin development guide is the main guide people follow when building plugins. It should feel like a real person teaching you, with genuine excitement about the developer experience and honest notes about gotchas.
acceptanceCriteria: Opening has personality matching the README's excitement about plugin building
The SDK utilities section conveys genuine enthusiasm — these save real time and the author is proud of them
The hot reload section conveys 'this is one of my favorite things' energy
Zod schema gotchas feel honest — 'here's where people get tripped up'
The 'Publish to npm' section echoes the README's 'recommend building over installing' philosophy
Error handling section links feel like personal recommendations, not cross-references
All code blocks, MDX components, tables, and links preserved exactly
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/guides/plugin-development.mdx (voice rework)

Context:
This is the longest guide (622 lines). Current opening: "I'll walk you through creating an OpenTabs plugin from scratch." — This is fine but bland compared to the README's "This is the part I'm most excited about."

README passages to channel:
- "Most of the plugins in this repo were built by AI in minutes."
- "The MCP server ships with site analysis tools, the SDK handles the boilerplate"
- "every time an AI builds a plugin, it writes what it learned back into the skill"

Key sections that need more personality:
1. The intro (line 7) — should match README energy about plugin building
2. SDK utilities intro (line 252): "I find myself reaching for these constantly" is good but could be stronger
3. Hot reload section (line 564): "This is one of the things I'm most happy with" is good but could channel the README's genuine excitement more
4. The Zod gotchas section (line 504): "There are a few gotchas to keep in mind" could be more honest like "Here's where I see people get stuck"
5. Publish section (line 577): should echo README's philosophy about building > installing

The page has a LOT of code blocks and tables — these should NOT change. Only the prose between them needs personality.

Constraints:
- Do NOT change any code blocks
- Do NOT change any <Table>, <Alert>, <PluginStructure />, <CliCommand /> components
- Do NOT change any links
- Focus prose changes on section intros and explanatory paragraphs
- Read the README at repo root as the gold standard for voice

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/guides/error-handling.mdx voice
userStories.description: The error handling guide explains a design decision (ToolError). It should feel like the author sharing why they built it this way — a personal design story, not a specification.
acceptanceCriteria: The intro feels like sharing a design decision — 'I noticed AI agents were guessing poorly at retry logic, so I built this'
The 'Problem with Unstructured Errors' section is visceral — the author has SEEN agents make bad retry decisions and it frustrated them
Factory methods feel like 'I designed these so you don't have to think about which category is which'
The error propagation section conveys 'this just works and you don't have to think about it'
The retry advice is honest — 'use the server's Retry-After header instead of making up a number'
All code blocks, MDX components (<ErrorCategories />), tables, and links preserved exactly
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/guides/error-handling.mdx (voice rework)

Context:
Current opening (line 7): "When a tool fails, how you report the error determines whether the AI agent retries intelligently, gives up, or tells the user something unhelpful. I built `ToolError` to solve this — it's a structured error class that carries machine-readable metadata through the entire dispatch chain to the AI agent."

This is decent but could be more personal. Compare to how the README handles the security section — "I know you're the kind of person who sets DANGEROUSLY_SKIP_PERMISSIONS=1 the moment something asks for confirmation. I respect that." That's the level of personality we want.

Key sections:
1. Intro (line 7) — needs more personal motivation
2. "The Problem with Unstructured Errors" (line 9) — the frustration of watching AI agents guess poorly at retries should come through
3. "in my experience, it usually guesses poorly" (line 17) — this is good, could be stronger
4. Factory methods intro (around line 114) — "I designed these so you don't have to remember" is good but could feel more genuine
5. Error propagation section — should convey 'I made this work end-to-end so you don't have to think about it'

Constraints:
- Do NOT change code blocks, <ErrorCategories />, <Table> components, or links
- Focus on prose paragraphs and section intros
- Read the README as the gold standard for voice

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/guides/streaming-progress.mdx voice
userStories.description: The streaming/progress guide explains timeout design decisions. It should feel like the author sharing engineering tradeoffs they thought hard about.
acceptanceCriteria: The timeout explanation feels like a genuine engineering tradeoff — 'I chose 30 seconds because X, and here's why that's right for most cases but not all'
The 5-minute ceiling rationale feels honest — 'anything longer is probably a sign your tool should be broken up'
The progress flow explanation acknowledges the complexity — 'there are a lot of hops, I know' while explaining why each hop exists
The context parameter explanation feels practical — 'I made it optional so your tests work without mocking'
All code blocks, MDX components (<ProgressFlow />), tables, and links preserved exactly
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/guides/streaming-progress.mdx (voice rework)

Context:
Current opening (line 7): "Long-running tools — bulk exports, multi-page scrapes, slow API calls — risk timing out before they finish. Progress reporting solves this: your tool tells the platform 'I'm still working' at each step, the dispatch timeout resets, and the AI agent sees live progress updates."

This is clear but could have more personality. The timeout explanation (lines 11-13) already has good personal voice: "I set it there because most tool calls should be fast" and "I picked 5 minutes as the ceiling because" — but these could be even more personal/honest.

Key opportunities:
1. The timeout choice could reference real experience — 'I watched tools hang for 2 minutes before realizing they were stuck'
2. The progress flow section could acknowledge the complexity honestly
3. The MCP client integration could note 'you generally don't need to think about this part'

Constraints:
- Do NOT change code blocks, <ProgressFlow />, <Table>, or links
- Focus on prose
- Read the README as gold standard

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/guides/logging-debugging.mdx voice
userStories.description: The logging/debugging guide should feel like the author sharing their personal debugging workflow — 'here's what I do when things break at 2am'.
acceptanceCriteria: The opening conveys 'I built this logging pipeline because I kept flying blind' energy
The debugging workflow feels like personal experience — 'here's the order I work through problems, and most issues reveal themselves by step 2'
Tool handler logging section conveys 'these log lines are the only thing standing between you and blindly guessing'
The audit section feels like 'this is usually where the answer is'
Best practices feel earned from real experience, not theoretical
All code blocks, MDX components, tables, and links preserved exactly
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/guides/logging-debugging.mdx (voice rework)

Context:
Current opening (line 7): "When a tool fails or does something unexpected, you need to figure out what happened. I built a logging pipeline that routes messages from your plugin code all the way to the MCP server, and a set of CLI tools to poke at the system when things aren't working. Here's how I use them."

This is decent but still reads like documentation. Compare to the README's direct, honest tone. The debugging guide should feel like a seasoned engineer sharing war stories, not a procedure manual.

Key sections that need personality:
1. Opening — should convey the frustration of debugging without logs
2. Tool handler logging section — should feel like 'trust me, add these logs or you'll regret it'
3. Debugging workflow — should feel like personal experience, not a checklist
4. The audit/doctor/DevTools progression — should feel like 'here's my actual debugging flow'
5. Best practices — should feel earned, not theoretical

Constraints:
- Do NOT change code blocks, <Table>, or links
- Focus on prose
- Read the README as gold standard
